s: Rhenium is a chemical element with symbol Re and atomic number 75, one of the rarest elements in Earth's crust.
q: What is rhenium named after?
e: Rhenium was the last stable element to be discovered, in 1925 in Germany. Named after the Rhine river, it has the third-highest melting point of all elements (3,186°C). Rhenium is primarily used in jet engine superalloys where extreme heat resistance is required.

s: Rhodium is a chemical element with symbol Rh and atomic number 45, the most expensive precious metal commonly traded.
q: What does 'rhodium' mean in Greek?
e: Rhodium is extremely rare — about 30 tonnes are mined annually worldwide. Its name comes from the Greek 'rhodon' meaning rose, due to the rose-red color of its compounds. Rhodium is used to coat white gold jewelry and in catalytic converters. It reached a record price of over $29,000 per ounce in 2021.

s: Rogue waves — single ocean waves over 25 meters tall that appear from nowhere and vanish just as quickly — were considered sailors' myths until one was scientifically measured hitting an oil platform in 1995.
q: What was the name of the oil platform where the first scientifically measured rogue wave was recorded in 1995?
e: The Draupner wave, recorded on January 1, 1995, at the Draupner oil platform in the North Sea, was 25.6 meters high in a sea state with average waves of only 12 meters. This confirmed that rogue waves are real and can be more than twice the height of surrounding waves. They're now thought to be caused by constructive interference of multiple wave trains.

s: Natural latex is essentially tree blood — rubber trees produce it as a wound-healing response, and harvesters must carefully cut the bark at a precise angle to collect it without killing the tree.
q: Why do rubber trees produce latex?
e: Latex flows through specialized vessels called laticifers in the inner bark of Hevea brasiliensis. Tappers make a thin spiral cut at 25-30 degrees from horizontal, and the latex drips into collection cups for 4-6 hours. Each tree can produce latex for 25-30 years.

s: Rubble masonry is a construction technique using rough, uncut stones set in mortar, one of the oldest building methods still in use.
q: What distinguishes rubble masonry from dressed masonry?
a: Rubble masonry uses rough, uncut stones
d: Rubble is a type of stone
It is inferior to other techniques
It cannot be used for tall structures
It was invented in the 19th century
e: Rubble masonry has been used for thousands of years because it requires no stone cutting — builders simply fit irregularly shaped stones together with mortar. Ancient Roman walls, medieval castles, and many rural buildings worldwide use this technique. It remains popular in earthquake-prone regions because it flexes rather than shattering.

s: Rubrication is the use of red letters for emphasis in manuscripts.
q: What color defines rubrication in manuscripts?
e: The word rubric still means a heading or rule today!.

s: Ruthenium is a chemical element with symbol Ru and atomic number 44, a rare platinum-group metal named after Russia.
q: What country is ruthenium named after?
e: Ruthenium was discovered in 1844 and named after Ruthenia, the Latin name for Russia. It is one of the rarest elements on Earth and belongs to the platinum group. Ruthenium is used in electronics, solar cells, and as a catalyst in chemical processes.

s: Salmon can smell their way home — they navigate thousands of kilometers back to the exact stream where they were born by following a unique chemical 'fingerprint' imprinted in their brains as juveniles.
q: How do salmon find their way back to the exact stream where they were born?
a: By smelling the stream's unique chemical signature
e: Young salmon (smolts) imprint on the specific blend of dissolved minerals, soil chemistry, and organic compounds in their natal stream. During their ocean migration years later, they detect these chemicals at concentrations as low as one part per billion, following the scent gradient upstream to spawn.

s: If all the salt in the world's oceans were extracted and spread evenly over Earth's land surface, it would form a layer about 150 meters (500 feet) thick — roughly the height of a 40-story building.
q: How thick would a layer of ocean salt be if spread over all of Earth's land?
a: About 150 meters — the height of a 40-story building
e: The oceans contain approximately 50 quadrillion tons (5 × 10¹⁶ kg) of dissolved salt, primarily sodium chloride. At an average salinity of 3.5%, every cubic kilometer of seawater holds about 36 million tons of salt. Earth's total land area is about 150 million km².

s: Saturn would float in water if you could find a bathtub big enough — it's the only planet in our solar system less dense than water, with a density of just 0.687 g/cm³.
q: What is Saturn's average density compared to water?
d: More dense than water — about 1.5 g/cm³
About the same density as water
Jupiter is also less dense than water
Only ice giants float in water
e: Saturn's low density is because it's composed mostly of hydrogen and helium gas. Despite being the second-largest planet (9.5 times Earth's diameter), its density is only about 69% that of water. For comparison, Earth's density is 5.51 g/cm³. Of course, Saturn wouldn't actually float intact — it would break apart, and its rocky core is denser than water.

s: Sawali is a woven bamboo material used in Filipino nipa huts.
q: What is sawali woven from?
e: Nipa huts withstand tropical storms thanks to flexible sawali!.

s: Scandium is a chemical element with symbol Sc and atomic number 21, a rare earth metal named after Scandinavia.
q: What region is scandium named after?
e: Scandium is classified as a rare earth element despite being relatively abundant in Earth's crust. It is used in aluminum-scandium alloys that are lighter and stronger, making them ideal for aerospace and sports equipment. Scandium was predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-boron' before its discovery in 1879.

s: A seed ball is a small sphere of clay mixed with seeds, compost, and sometimes chili powder, used for no-till reforestation.
q: Who popularized the modern seed ball technique?
e: Seed balls were popularized by Japanese farmer Masanobu Fukuoka as part of natural farming. The clay protects seeds from birds and harsh weather until rains soften it. Chili powder is sometimes added to deter animals. Seed balls have been used to reforest deserts and restore degraded landscapes by simply throwing them onto bare ground.

s: Sheet moulding compound (SMC) is so strong that car body panels made from it can be lighter than steel while surviving crash forces — and unlike metal, SMC panels never rust.
q: Why is sheet moulding compound (SMC) used in car body panels?
a: It is lighter than steel, strong, and rust-proof
e: SMC consists of chopped glass fibers (25-50mm long) sandwiched in polyester resin between carrier films. When compression-molded at 150°C, it produces parts with high strength-to-weight ratios used in automotive body panels, electrical housings, and satellite dishes.

s: Silver is a chemical element with symbol Ag and atomic number 47, and is the best electrical conductor of all elements.
q: What is the chemical symbol for silver?
e: Silver has the highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any element. Its chemical symbol Ag comes from the Latin word 'argentum.' Silver has been used as currency, in jewelry, and in photography for centuries. It also has natural antimicrobial properties.

s: The longest animal ever recorded is not a whale — it's a siphonophore, a colonial organism made of thousands of tiny interconnected individuals. One specimen measured 46 meters long, far exceeding any blue whale.
q: What is the longest animal ever recorded?
a: A siphonophore — a colonial marine organism
e: In 2020, scientists aboard the research vessel Falkor discovered an Apolemia siphonophore in a canyon off Western Australia that measured approximately 46 meters. Siphonophores are colonies of specialized zooids — each individual performs a specific function (feeding, swimming, reproduction, defense) but cannot survive alone. The Portuguese man-of-war is a well-known siphonophore.

s: Every arm of a single snowflake is nearly identical to the others because all six arms experience the same atmospheric conditions as they fall — but no two separate snowflakes follow the same path, so no two are truly alike.
q: Why are all six arms of a single snowflake nearly identical to each other?
a: They all experience the same atmospheric conditions as the crystal falls
d: Each arm grows independently from different clouds
Temperature varies across the falling crystal
Each arm experiences different humidity levels
The crystal rotates as it falls
Wind currents affect each arm differently
e: Snowflakes form as water vapor deposits onto an ice crystal. The crystal's hexagonal structure (from the angle of hydrogen bonds in water) ensures six-fold symmetry. Since all arms of one crystal experience identical temperature, humidity, and airflow as they fall, they grow identically. But the path through the atmosphere is unique to each crystal, creating unique patterns.

s: The Sun delivers about 1,361 watts per square meter to Earth's upper atmosphere — enough energy hitting our planet every hour to power human civilization for an entire year.
q: Approximately how much solar power reaches Earth's upper atmosphere per square meter?
e: The solar constant (approximately 1,361 W/m²) is the amount of incoming electromagnetic radiation per unit area at Earth's distance from the Sun. When multiplied by Earth's cross-sectional area, the total energy received in just one hour exceeds the total annual global energy consumption.

s: In space, no one can hear you scream — not because there is no sound, but because sound is a pressure wave that needs a medium (gas, liquid, or solid) to travel through, and space is nearly a perfect vacuum with almost no particles to carry vibrations.
q: Why can sound not travel through outer space?
a: Space is nearly a vacuum with too few particles to carry pressure waves
d: Sound waves are too short to travel far
Temperature is too cold for vibrations
Space is too empty for amplification
e: Sound requires molecules to compress and expand in waves. Space has about 1 atom per cubic centimeter (compared to 25 quintillion per cm³ in air at sea level). However, in dense nebulae or planetary atmospheres, sound can propagate — NASA even translated pressure waves from a black hole into audible sound.

s: Sound travels about 15 times faster through steel than through air — a train track transmits sound so efficiently that you can hear a train approaching by pressing your ear to the rail long before you hear it through the air.
q: How much faster does sound travel through steel compared to air?
e: Sound speed depends on the medium's stiffness and density. Steel's elastic modulus is enormous compared to air's compressibility. Sound travels at about 343 m/s in air but about 5,120 m/s in steel — nearly 6,000 km/h.

s: Gravity travels at exactly the speed of light — if the Sun vanished, Earth would keep orbiting for 8 minutes and 20 seconds, the same delay as the last ray of sunlight reaching us.
q: How fast does gravity propagate through space?
e: Einstein's general relativity predicts that changes in gravitational fields propagate at c (the speed of light). This was confirmed by LIGO's detection of gravitational waves in 2015, which arrived at light speed from merging black holes 1.3 billion light-years away.

s: Spider silk is five times stronger than steel by weight and three times tougher than Kevlar — if you could make a pencil-thick strand, it could stop a Boeing 747 in flight.
q: How does spider silk compare to steel in terms of strength-to-weight ratio?
a: Spider silk is about five times stronger than steel by weight
d: Spider silk is weaker than steel
Spider silk is two times stronger
Spider silk is ten times stronger
Spider silk is equal to Kevlar
Spider silk is weaker than Kevlar
Spider silk cannot be measured accurately
e: Darwin's bark spider produces the toughest biological material ever studied. Spider silk's strength comes from a combination of crystalline protein regions (providing strength) connected by amorphous regions (providing elasticity). It can stretch up to 40% of its length without breaking. Despite decades of research, scientists still cannot fully replicate it synthetically.

s: Stainless steel contains chromium which prevents rust and corrosion.
q: What element makes stainless steel rust-resistant?
e: Stainless steel was accidentally discovered in 1913!.

s: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches — current estimates put the star count at roughly 200 sextillion (2 × 10²³), compared to about 7.5 quintillion (7.5 × 10¹⁸) grains of sand.
q: Are there more stars in the universe or grains of sand on Earth's beaches?
a: Stars — about 26,000 times more stars than sand grains
d: Sand grains by a million times more
Stars by a thousand times more
e: Astronomers estimate about 2 trillion galaxies in the observable universe, each containing roughly 100 billion stars on average. Earth's beaches contain an estimated 7.5 × 10¹⁸ grains of sand. This makes the star count roughly 26,000 times larger than the grain count.

s: Your stomach acid (hydrochloric acid) has a pH of 1-2, strong enough to dissolve metal — your stomach must completely replace its lining every 3-4 days to avoid digesting itself.
q: How often does the stomach completely replace its protective lining?
e: The parietal cells of the stomach lining produce concentrated HCl. The stomach protects itself with a thick layer of mucus containing bicarbonate, creating a pH gradient from 1-2 in the lumen to 7 at the cell surface. If this mucus barrier breaks down (from H. pylori infection or excessive NSAID use), the acid creates ulcers.

s: Botanically, a strawberry is not a berry — but a banana, an avocado, and a watermelon are. In botany, a true berry must develop from a single ovary of a single flower.
q: Which of these is a true botanical berry?
e: A strawberry is actually an 'accessory fruit' because the fleshy part grows from the receptacle, not the ovary. Each tiny 'seed' on the surface is the real fruit (an achene). Bananas, avocados, and watermelons all develop from a single ovary, making them true berries.

s: The Eiffel Tower was one of the first major structures built with structural steel's predecessor — wrought iron — and contains 7,300 tons of it, held together by 2.5 million rivets.
q: How many rivets hold the Eiffel Tower together?
e: Structural steel, with its precisely controlled carbon content, largely replaced wrought iron by the 1890s. The Eiffel Tower, completed in 1889, sits at the transition point between these two materials and was originally intended as a temporary structure.

s: Sugan is an Irish straw rope that was so central to rural life that entire chairs were woven from it — and making sugan was considered a communal evening activity, like storytelling by the fire.
q: What is sugan?
e: Sugan (from Irish 'sugán') was twisted from wheat or oat straw and used for everything from thatching roofs to tying haystacks. Sugan chairs, with seats woven from these ropes, remain iconic symbols of Irish rural heritage.

s: Sulfur is a chemical element with symbol S and atomic number 16, known since antiquity as 'brimstone.'
q: What is the ancient name for sulfur?
e: Sulfur is mentioned in the Bible as brimstone and has been used since ancient times. It is essential for life — found in the amino acids cysteine and methionine. Sulfur is bright yellow in its solid form and produces a distinctive rotten-egg smell as hydrogen sulfide. Volcanic hot springs often contain dissolved sulfur.

s: The light hitting your skin right now was created in the Sun's core about 100,000 years ago — photons bounce around inside the Sun so many times (a 'random walk') that it takes millennia to reach the surface, then only 8 minutes to reach Earth.
q: How long does it take a photon created in the Sun's core to reach its surface?
e: In the Sun's dense interior, photons are absorbed and re-emitted billions of times, traveling only a few millimeters between collisions. This random walk from core to surface takes an estimated 10,000 to 170,000 years. Once the photon escapes the surface, it travels to Earth in just 8 minutes and 20 seconds.

s: When helium is cooled below -271°C, it becomes a superfluid that can climb walls, flow through solid ceramics, and create fountains that never stop — it literally has zero viscosity.
q: What bizarre property does helium gain when cooled below -271°C?
a: It becomes a superfluid with zero viscosity
d: It turns into a solid immediately
It becomes magnetic and repels metal
It hardens like diamond under pressure
It becomes transparent to all radiation
e: Superfluid helium-4 (He II) exhibits quantum mechanical effects on a macroscopic scale. Below the lambda point (2.17 K), it flows without any friction whatsoever. It can creep up container walls as a thin film, squeeze through pores too small for ordinary liquids, and even create a perpetual fountain if heated from below.

s: Tardigrades can survive being boiled, frozen to near absolute zero, exposed to the vacuum of space, and blasted with radiation 1,000 times the lethal human dose — all by essentially turning themselves into glass.
q: What sugar do tardigrades produce to survive extreme conditions by vitrifying their cells?
e: When threatened, tardigrades enter a state called cryptobiosis: they expel almost all water from their bodies and produce a special sugar called trehalose that vitrifies (turns to glass) around their cells, preserving their structure. They can remain in this state for decades and revive when rehydrated.

s: In 2007, tardigrades became the first animals to survive exposure to outer space — they were sent outside a spacecraft for 10 days, enduring vacuum, cosmic radiation, and UV bombardment, and most survived and even laid viable eggs afterward.
q: What survival feat did tardigrades accomplish in outer space in 2007?
a: Survived 10 days of vacuum, radiation, and UV outside a spacecraft
d: Survived 1 hour but all died afterward
Could not survive any exposure to space
Survived but with permanent genetic damage
e: The TARDIS experiment on the ESA Foton-M3 mission exposed tardigrades to the full space environment. They survived by entering cryptobiosis, replacing body water with a glass-like sugar called trehalose, and producing unique damage-suppressing proteins.

s: The 'tongue map' showing sweet at the tip, sour on the sides, and bitter at the back is a myth — all taste buds on your tongue can detect all five basic tastes equally well.
q: Can all areas of the tongue detect all five basic tastes?
a: Yes — the tongue map is a myth
d: No, only the tip detects sweetness
No, only the back detects bitterness
No, the sides only detect saltiness
No, each region has specialized taste receptors
No, most taste buds are on the back
No, taste buds are not evenly distributed
e: The myth originated from a mistranslation of a 1901 German thesis by D.P. Hanig. While there are minor sensitivity differences, every region of the tongue has receptors for sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami. The map was definitively debunked by Virginia Collings in 1974.

s: Thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) is a versatile elastic polymer used in phone cases, shoe soles, and medical devices.
q: What unique property distinguishes TPU from regular rubber?
a: It can be melted and reformed repeatedly
d: It cannot be recycled or reshaped
It requires chemical solvents to form
e: TPU combines the elasticity of rubber with the processability of plastic — it can be melted and reformed repeatedly without losing its properties. It is resistant to oil, grease, and abrasion, making it ideal for protective cases and industrial applications. TPU is also used in 3D printing to create flexible parts.

s: Your tongue print is completely unique — just like fingerprints, no two people have the same combination of tongue shape, texture, and taste bud patterns, and researchers are developing tongue-based biometric identification systems.
q: What body part has a unique print like a fingerprint?
e: The tongue's geometric shape, physiological texture, and distribution of papillae (which house taste buds) create a print as distinctive as a fingerprint. Unlike fingers, tongues are harder to forge and are protected inside the mouth.

s: Trachycarpus is a genus of cold-hardy fan palm trees from Asia.
q: What leaf shape do Trachycarpus palms have?
e: Trachycarpus palms survive temperatures as low as minus 15 degrees!.

s: Tree rings recorded an enormous burst of cosmic radiation in 774 AD — a mysterious spike of carbon-14 found in trees worldwide, possibly from a nearby gamma-ray burst or an extreme solar storm far more powerful than any recorded.
q: What did scientists find in tree rings from 774 AD?
a: A massive spike of cosmic radiation (carbon-14)
e: Japanese physicist Fusa Miyake discovered the anomaly in 2012 by analyzing carbon-14 in Japanese cedar rings. The event deposited 20 times the normal annual carbon-14 in a single year. The cause remains debated, but the event — now called the 'Miyake Event' — was 100 times more powerful than the 1859 Carrington Event.

s: Trees under attack by insects release volatile chemicals that warn neighboring trees to preemptively produce defensive toxins — essentially screaming for help through the air.
q: How do trees warn their neighbors about insect attacks?
a: By releasing volatile chemical signals into the air
d: Through electrical pulses in the soil
e: When herbivorous insects damage leaves, trees release volatile organic compounds (VOCs) like methyl jasmonate. Nearby trees detect these airborne signals and ramp up production of tannins, alkaloids, and other chemical defenses before the insects even reach them. Some trees also release chemicals that attract the insects' predators.

s: Forests make their own rain — trees release volatile organic compounds that seed cloud formation, and the Amazon rainforest generates up to 50% of its own rainfall through this process.
q: How do forests contribute to their own rainfall?
a: Trees release chemicals that seed cloud formation
d: Trees absorb moisture from soil and release it as rain
Trees have natural pressure systems that force rain clouds down
Tree roots push groundwater upward creating humidity
Trees create static electricity that attracts cloud
Leaf transpiration somehow causes water to fall
Trees generate heat that lifts water vapor directly
e: Trees emit isoprene and terpenes that oxidize in the atmosphere to form aerosol particles. These particles act as cloud condensation nuclei, triggering cloud formation and precipitation. Deforestation can therefore reduce regional rainfall in a feedback loop.

s: There are individual trees alive today that are older than the Egyptian pyramids — the oldest known living tree, a Great Basin bristlecone pine named Methuselah, has been alive for over 4,856 years.
q: What species is the oldest known living non-clonal tree on Earth?
e: Methuselah germinated around 2833 BCE, making it older than the Great Pyramid of Giza (built c. 2560 BCE). Its exact location in California's White Mountains is kept secret to prevent vandalism. An even older bristlecone pine was confirmed in 2012 at 5,065 years old, but remains unnamed. These trees survive in harsh, high-altitude environments where few competitors or pathogens can thrive.

s: Tall trees pump water from their roots to their highest leaves without any mechanical pump — they use the evaporation of water from leaves to create a suction force that pulls water up through microscopic tubes against gravity, sometimes over 100 meters high.
q: What force primarily drives water from the roots to the top of tall trees?
a: Transpiration pull — evaporation from leaves creating suction
d: Root pressure from the soil pushing upward
Capillary action alone through the wood
A mechanical pump-like structure in the wood
Gravity pulling water down through the trunk
Root cells actively pumping water molecule by molecule
Solar radiation heating the xylem tube
e: This process, called transpiration pull, works because water molecules are cohesive (they stick together via hydrogen bonds) and adhesive (they stick to the tube walls). As water evaporates from leaf stomata, it creates negative pressure that pulls a continuous chain of water molecules up through the xylem. The tension can reach -2 MPa in tall trees.

s: Unbihexium is a hypothetical chemical element with atomic number 126, theorized to lie near the center of the 'island of stability.'
q: Why is element 126 of special interest to nuclear physicists?
a: 126 protons may be a nuclear 'magic number' for stability
d: It is relatively easy to synthesize
126 protons would make it highly unstable
It has already been discovered and named
114 is the predicted magic number, not 126
Its name is officially recognized by IUPAC
e: Element 126 is particularly interesting because nuclear physics models predict that 126 protons may form a 'magic number' — a especially stable configuration. If created, unbihexium might have a half-life of minutes or even years instead of the milliseconds typical of superheavy elements. This would be a breakthrough in nuclear physics.

s: Unbinilium is a hypothetical chemical element with atomic number 120 that has never been successfully synthesized.
q: What is the atomic number of the hypothetical element unbinilium?
e: Unbinilium (element 120) would be an alkaline earth metal in period 8 of the periodic table if created. Multiple laboratories have attempted to synthesize it by bombarding heavier elements with calcium or titanium ions, but none have succeeded. Its creation would help scientists understand the 'island of stability' hypothesis.

s: Unbiunium is a hypothetical chemical element with atomic number 121, predicted to be the first element in the superactinide series.
q: What new electron orbital would unbiunium be the first to use?
e: Element 121 would begin a new series of elements called the superactinides, where 5g electron orbitals start to fill for the first time. This would be completely new territory in chemistry, as no 5g orbital has ever been observed in any atom. Relativistic effects would make unbiunium's chemistry very different from lighter elements.

s: Unhexhexium is a hypothetical chemical element with atomic number 166, one of the most speculative entries on the extended periodic table.
q: What extreme phenomenon might prevent very heavy elements like 166 from existing?
a: Electron-positron pair creation from the nuclear field
d: It would become magnetic and repel itself
The nucleus would collapse into a black hole
It would emit so much heat it would vaporize
e: Element 166 is so far beyond current synthesis capabilities that its properties can only be estimated using quantum mechanical calculations. Some models suggest elements this heavy might not form stable atoms at all, as the extreme nuclear charge could cause electron-positron pair creation. The extended periodic table remains one of chemistry's great frontiers.

s: Unpentpentium is a hypothetical chemical element with atomic number 155, far beyond any element ever created.
q: How does the IUPAC systematic name 'unpentpentium' encode the atomic number?
e: Unpentpentium's systematic name follows IUPAC nomenclature rules: un (1) + pent (5) + pent (5) + ium = element 155. The heaviest element ever synthesized is oganesson (118), so element 155 remains purely theoretical. If it exists, it may have properties unlike any known element due to extreme relativistic effects on its electrons.

s: Unquadhexium is a hypothetical chemical element with atomic number 146, near the predicted 'island of stability' for superheavy elements.
q: What is the atomic number of unquadhexium?
e: Element 146 has 146 protons and would need around 190-200 neutrons for maximum stability. Some nuclear models predict that elements near atomic number 146 could have significantly longer half-lives than neighboring elements due to shell closure effects. Creating such an element remains far beyond current technology.

s: Vanadium is a hard transition metal with symbol V and atomic number 23.
q: What is vanadium's chemical symbol?
e: This fact highlights an important aspect of the topic worth remembering.

s: Venus rotates backwards compared to every other planet in the solar system — and so slowly that a single day on Venus (243 Earth days) is longer than its year (225 Earth days).
q: What is unusual about the direction of Venus's rotation?
a: It rotates backwards (retrograde) compared to most planets
d: It rotates in the same direction as Earth but very slowly
It alternates between forward and backward rotation
It rotates at an extreme tilt
It rotates perfectly aligned with its orbit
e: Venus has retrograde rotation, meaning it spins clockwise when viewed from above its north pole (opposite to Earth and most other planets). The leading theory is that a massive collision early in its history flipped it over. Its extremely slow rotation also means the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east on Venus.
